title: Witch Week - Chapter 14 (Fragment 3)
text:
Then it turned out that Chrestomanci wanted to see them in reverse alphabetical order. They all had to march up and down and around again, so that Heather Young and Ronald West were at the front of the line instead of Geoffrey Barnes and Deborah Clifton. They did it with none of the usual grumbling and scuffling. Even Charles, who was quite certain that they were only marching to give Chrestomanci time to put all Nan’s inventions in, found himself a little quiet and queasy, with his thumb rubbing at that blister. Heather and Ronald looked quite ill with terror. Dan Smith—who was third now that Brian was missing—asked Nirupam in an urgent whisper, “What’s he going to do with us?”

Nirupam had no more idea than Dan. He had not even known that Chrestomanci really was going to question them. He tried to look sinister. “You’ll see.” Dan’s face went cream-colored.

Chrestomanci did not see people for the same length of time. Heather disappeared into the study for what seemed an endless age, and she came out as frightened as she went in. Ronald was only in for a minute, and he came out from behind the darkened door looking relieved. He leaned across Dan and Nirupam to whisper to Simon, “No problem at all!”

“I knew there wouldn’t be,” Simon lied loftily.

“Quiet!” bellowed Mr. Wentworth. “Next—Daniel Smith.”

Dan Smith was not gone long either, but he did not come out looking as if there were no problem. His face was more like cheese than cream.

Nirupam was gone for much longer than either Nan or Charles had expected. When he came out, he was frowning and uneasy. He was followed by Simon. There was another endless wait. During it, the bell rang for afternoon lessons, and was followed by the usual surge of hurrying feet. The silence of lessons which came after that had gone on for so long before Simon came out that there was not a soul in 6B who did not feel like an outcast. Simon came out at last. He was an odd color. He would not speak to any of the friends who were leaning out of the line wanting to know what had happened. He just walked to the wall like a sleepwalker and leaned against it, staring into space.

This did not make anyone feel better. Nan wondered what Chrestomanci was doing to people in there. By the time the three girls who came between her and Simon had all come out looking as bad as Heather Young, Nan was so scared that she could hardly make her legs work. But it was her turn. She had to go. She shuffled around the dark door somehow.

Inside, she stood and stared. Chrestomanci had indeed been very busy while 6B marched up and down outside. Mr. Wentworth’s study was entirely lined with black curtains. A black carpet Nan had forgotten to invent covered the parquet floor. Hung on the walls and glimmering against the black background were manacles, a noose, festoons of chains, several kinds of scourge, and a cat-o’-nine-tails. There was a large can in one corner labeled PETROL, DIV. INQ. OFFICE, FOR USE IN TORTURE ONLY.

Chrestomanci himself was only dimly visible behind a huge glittering lamp, which reminded Nan uncomfortably of the light over an operating table. The light from it beamed onto Mr. Wentworth’s desk, draped in more black cloth, where there was a sort of jeweler’s display of shiny thumbscrews and other displeasing objects. The wired-up helmet was there. So was a bouquet of bare wires, spitting blue sparks. Behind those was a pile of fat black books.

vocabulary.definition: To shout something with a deep loud roar.
vocabulary.example: ” bellowed Mr.

vocabulary.definition: A bubble on the skin caused by friction or burning.
vocabulary.example: Even Charles, who was quite certain that they were only marching to give Chrestomanci time to put all Nan’s inventions in, found himself a little quiet and queasy, with his thumb rubbing at that blister.

vocabulary.definition: A whip with nine knotted cords, used for flogging.
vocabulary.example: Hung on the walls and glimmering against the black background were manacles, a noose, festoons of chains, several kinds of scourge, and a cat-o’-nine-tails.

vocabulary.definition: A dairy product made from curdled milk, here used metaphorically for color description.
vocabulary.example: His face was more like cheese than cream.

vocabulary.definition: A proper name of a character, lexically important in the text.
vocabulary.example: Then it turned out that Chrestomanci wanted to see them in reverse alphabetical order.

vocabulary.definition: Having the pale yellowish-white color of cream.
vocabulary.example: ” Dan’s face went cream-colored.

vocabulary.definition: Causing dissatisfaction or annoyance.
vocabulary.example: Wentworth’s desk, draped in more black cloth, where there was a sort of jeweler’s display of shiny thumbscrews and other displeasing objects.

vocabulary.definition: A very long or seemingly infinite period of waiting.
vocabulary.example: Heather disappeared into the study for what seemed an endless age, and she came out as frightened as she went in.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling fear or alarm.
vocabulary.example: Heather disappeared into the study for what seemed an endless age, and she came out as frightened as she went in.

vocabulary.definition: To furrow one's brows, showing displeasure or worry.
vocabulary.example: When he came out, he was frowning and uneasy.

vocabulary.definition: Shining faintly with a wavering light.
vocabulary.example: Hung on the walls and glimmering against the black background were manacles, a noose, festoons of chains, several kinds of scourge, and a cat-o’-nine-tails.

vocabulary.definition: A low sound expressing dissatisfaction or complaint.
vocabulary.example: They did it with none of the usual grumbling and scuffling.

vocabulary.definition: A case or arrangement presenting jewelry or small valuable objects.
vocabulary.example: Wentworth’s desk, draped in more black cloth, where there was a sort of jeweler’s display of shiny thumbscrews and other displeasing objects.

vocabulary.definition: A device for giving light.
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci himself was only dimly visible behind a huge glittering lamp, which reminded Nan uncomfortably of the light over an operating table.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows superiority or an attitude of being above others.
vocabulary.example:
”

“I knew there wouldn’t be,” Simon lied loftily.

vocabulary.definition: Metal restraints for hands or wrists.
vocabulary.example: Hung on the walls and glimmering against the black background were manacles, a noose, festoons of chains, several kinds of scourge, and a cat-o’-nine-tails.

vocabulary.definition: A loop with a running knot, used to trap or hang.
vocabulary.example: Hung on the walls and glimmering against the black background were manacles, a noose, festoons of chains, several kinds of scourge, and a cat-o’-nine-tails.

vocabulary.definition: A person rejected by society or a group.
vocabulary.example: The silence of lessons which came after that had gone on for so long before Simon came out that there was not a soul in 6B who did not feel like an outcast.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling sick to the stomach; nauseated.
vocabulary.example: Even Charles, who was quite certain that they were only marching to give Chrestomanci time to put all Nan’s inventions in, found himself a little quiet and queasy, with his thumb rubbing at that blister.

vocabulary.definition: No longer feeling distressed or anxious.
vocabulary.example: Ronald was only in for a minute, and he came out from behind the darkened door looking relieved.

vocabulary.definition: An ordering of items starting from the end of the alphabet backwards.
vocabulary.example: Then it turned out that Chrestomanci wanted to see them in reverse alphabetical order.

vocabulary.definition: A whip used as an instrument of punishment.
vocabulary.example: Hung on the walls and glimmering against the black background were manacles, a noose, festoons of chains, several kinds of scourge, and a cat-o’-nine-tails.

vocabulary.definition: A short confused fight or struggle at close quarters.
vocabulary.example: They did it with none of the usual grumbling and scuffling.

vocabulary.definition: To walk by dragging one's feet along without lifting them fully.
vocabulary.example: She shuffled around the dark door somehow.

vocabulary.definition: Giving the impression that something harmful or evil is happening or will happen.
vocabulary.example: He tried to look sinister.

vocabulary.definition: A person who walks about while asleep.
vocabulary.example: He just walked to the wall like a sleepwalker and leaned against it, staring into space.

vocabulary.definition: Emitting visible bursts or flashes of blue electrical sparks.
vocabulary.example: So was a bouquet of bare wires, spitting blue sparks.

vocabulary.definition: Extreme fear.
vocabulary.example: Heather and Ronald looked quite ill with terror.

vocabulary.definition: A torture device used to inflict pain by compressing the thumbs.
vocabulary.example: Wentworth’s desk, draped in more black cloth, where there was a sort of jeweler’s display of shiny thumbscrews and other displeasing objects.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling anxious or uncomfortable.
vocabulary.example: When he came out, he was frowning and uneasy.

vocabulary.definition: A soft, hushed voice expressing urgency.
vocabulary.example: Dan Smith—who was third now that Brian was missing—asked Nirupam in an urgent whisper, “What’s he going to do with us?

vocabulary.definition: A helmet connected with wires, likely for some technical or experimental purpose.
vocabulary.example: The wired-up helmet was there.
